input.prompt: Review this text truncation library for performance. The truncation methods are called on every displayed string in a content delivery system serving thousands of concurrent users.

data.content: Review this text truncation library for performance. The truncation methods are called on every displayed string in a content delivery system serving thousands of concurrent users.

<current_datetime>2026-04-22T01:08:38.579Z</current_datetime>

Review this text truncation library for performance. The truncation methods are called on every displayed string in a content delivery system serving thousands of concurrent users.

input.initialPrompt: Review this text truncation library for performance. The truncation methods are called on every displayed string in a content delivery system serving thousands of concurrent users.

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to review a C# file for performance issues. Let me start by reading the file.
Let me also check if there's a `StringReadabilityExtensions` class referenced in the code.

data.content:
Here's my performance review. There are **5 significant issues** for a hot path serving thousands of concurrent users:

---

### 1. `FixedLengthTruncator.Truncate` — double allocation on right-truncate (line 72)
```csharp
return value[..(length - truncationString.Length)].TrimEnd() + truncationString;
```
The range `value[..n]` allocates a substring, then `TrimEnd()` may allocate another, then `+` allocates a third. **3 potential allocations** per call. Use `AsSpan` + `string.Concat` like the left-truncate branch already does, trimming via span index arithmetic.

---

### 2. `FixedNumberOfWordsTruncator` — `Split()` allocates an entire `string[]` (line 178)
```csharp
var words = value.Split(' ', StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries);
```
This allocates an array **and** a new string per word on every call — even if the string is already short enough. For a hot path, use `MemoryExtensions.Count` or a span-based word walker to count words first, and only split (or walk again to build the result) when truncation is actually needed.

---

### 3. `FixedNumberOfWordsTruncator` — LINQ `Take`/`Skip` + `string.Join` (lines 187, 190)
```csharp
string.Join(" ", words.Take(length)) + truncationString;
```
`Take`/`Skip` create iterator objects; `string.Join` with an `IEnumerable` can't pre-size its `StringBuilder`. After splitting, use `Array.AsSpan` slicing (`words[..length]`) which avoids the LINQ overhead and lets `string.Join` use the array-optimized overload.

---

### 4. `Symbols.IsSymbol` / `GetSymbolGroup` — O(n) scan through `List<char>[]` (lines 259–287)
Each call iterates up to 5 lists totaling ~28 elements via `List<char>.Contains` (linear scan). At thousands of calls per request this adds up. Replace with a `FrozenSet<char>`, a `SearchValues<char>` (.NET 8+), or a simple 128-entry `bool[]` lookup table for **O(1)** character classification.

---

### 5. `Symbols.SymbolGroups` — heap-allocated `List<char>[]` for static data (line 244)
```csharp
internal static readonly List<char>[] SymbolGroups = [ ... ];
```
These are immutable lookup data. `List<char>` allocates a backing array, list object, and `char[]` per group — all on the heap. Use `ReadOnlyMemory<char>[]` or flat `char[]` arrays with `Array.AsReadOnly`, or (best) a flat `SearchValues<char>` to collapse everything into a single cache-friendly structure.

---

### Summary

| # | Location | Issue | Impact |
|---|----------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `FixedLengthTruncator` L72 | 3 string allocations per right-truncate | High — GC pressure |
| 2 | `FixedNumberOfWordsTruncator` L178 | `Split()` allocates even when no-op | High — wasteful alloc |
| 3 | `FixedNumberOfWordsTruncator` L187,190 | LINQ iterators + unoptimized `Join` | Medium — avoidable overhead |
| 4 | `Symbols.IsSymbol`/`GetSymbolGroup` | O(n) linear scan per char | Medium–High at scale |
| 5 | `Symbols.SymbolGroups` | Heap `List<char>` for static data | Low–Medium — one-time but wasteful |

The highest-priority fixes are **#1** and **#2** — they create garbage on every single call in the hot path. Issues #3–#5 compound under load.
